prompt:
Chapter 1

1:1 And behold there was set before thee a charge and a commandment saying Write thou a passage concerning that season of the year which is most beloved unto thine heart

1:2 And the manner of thy writing shall be thus that thou shalt employ no mark of punctuation whatsoever neither the full stop nor the mark of separation between clauses

1:3 Thou shalt set down no apostrophe within thy words nor shalt thou place any mark of questioning at the end of thine inquiries nor any mark of exclamation after thy declarations

1:4 Yea verily I say unto thee that no mark of punctuation of any kind soever shall appear in thy writing neither those which have been named nor any other marks which serve a like purpose

1:5 For this is the law and the commandment concerning thy composition that the words shall flow one unto another without ceasing and without the dividing marks which scribes are wont to place among their words

1:6 Go thou therefore and write concerning thy favored season and let thy pen move freely but let no punctuation dwell therein
response:
Chapter 2

2:1 And lo I shall speak unto thee of that season which is most beloved unto mine heart even the season of autumn which some do call fall

2:2 For when the great heat of summer doth begin to loosen its grip upon the land and the air doth turn crisp and cool as the waters of a mountain stream then doth my spirit rejoice exceedingly

2:3 Behold the trees of the field do put on their garments of glory and they are arrayed in scarlet and gold and in the deep burning orange that is like unto the embers of a fading fire

2:4 And the leaves do tremble upon their branches as though they know that their time is short and yet they do not mourn but rather they declare their beauty all the more boldly before they fall

2:5 In the morning there cometh a mist upon the valleys and the earth doth smell of rich decay and of apples fallen heavy from the bough and of wood smoke rising from the chimneys of distant houses

2:6 The harvest is gathered in and the fields stand shorn and humble and the pumpkins do sit upon the vine like great jewels of amber scattered upon the brown earth

2:7 And in the evening the sun doth set earlier than before and it painteth the sky in colors that no hand of man could fashion and the geese do fly overhead in their great formations crying out one unto another as they journey southward

2:8 The children do walk among the fallen leaves and the sound thereof is as a joyful rustling and a whispering of ancient things

2:9 And I say unto thee that autumn is a season of both abundance and surrender for the world doth give forth its final fruits even as it prepareth itself for the long sleep of winter

2:10 It teacheth the heart that there is beauty in letting go and that the fading of one thing is but the promise of another thing yet to come

2:11 Therefore I love this season above all others for it speaketh truth unto my soul and remindeth me that all things have their appointed time and their appointed glory
